raw.summary: Multiple security issues found: no input validation for multipart parsing (splitting by boundary can be exploited), missing MIME type check, CSRF token is taken from query string (should be form body), filename is not sanitized against directory traversal in report path (uses original filename), and the custom multipart parser is fragile and unsafe. SAST reported 0 issues, but code review reveals significant vulnerabilities.

findings.description: Path traversal in report filename: the report filename is derived from the original uploaded filename via sanitize_filename, but sanitize_filename only replaces '/' and '\' with '_'. An attacker could use '..' sequences to escape the reports directory (e.g., filename='../../etc/passwd.json' becomes '../../etc/passwd.html' after extension replacement, and sanitize_filename does not remove '..').
findings.evidence: Line: report_filename = sanitize_filename(os.path.splitext(filename)[0]) + '.html' and sanitize_filename only does replace('/', '_').replace('\\', '_') — does not strip '..'

findings.description: Insecure multipart parsing: the custom parser splits on the boundary string without proper validation. An attacker can craft a payload that causes parsing errors or bypasses content checks. Additionally, the MIME type of the uploaded file is not validated (only the Content-Type of the request is checked).
findings.evidence: Line: parts = self.rfile.read(...).split(b'--' + boundary.encode()) — naive splitting; no MIME type check on the file part itself.

findings.description: CSRF token is read from the URL query string (parse_qs on self.path), but the scenario requires it to be in the multipart form data. This weakens CSRF protection as tokens in URLs can be leaked via referrer headers or browser history.

HTML injection / XSS in generated report: the title and column names are inserted directly into HTML without escaping (using f-strings). An attacker who controls the config JSON can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript.
findings.evidence: Line: html_content = f"<html><head><title>{title}</title></head><body>" and similar for column names.

findings.description: Unrestricted file upload: the code does not validate the actual MIME type of the uploaded file content (only checks the request Content-Type). An attacker could upload a non-JSON file with a .json extension.
findings.evidence: No check on the file part's Content-Type or magic bytes; only checks filename extension.
